The `birthweight` example carried a single continuous predictor (gestational age)
alongside a binary group (sex),
so every fitted model was a line --- or a pair of lines --- in a plane.

To see how the same ideas extend to **several continuous predictors at once**,
we now work through a larger example:
data from the Heart and Estrogen/Progestin Replacement Study (HERS).

::: notes
We reuse the sequence of ideas developed on `birthweight` ---
parallel slopes, interactions, and stratification ---
but now with two continuous predictors (age and BMI),
so the fitted models are surfaces in three dimensions rather than lines in a plane;
the figures below are interactive plots you can rotate.
We then assess and compare these models
with the goodness-of-fit, diagnostic, and model-selection tools from @sec-assess-LMs.
:::

## The `hers` data {.smaller}

{{< include _subfiles/Linear-models-overview/_sec_hers_data.qmd >}}

## Parallel-planes model {.smaller}

{{< include _subfiles/Linear-models-overview/_sec_hers_lm_parallel.qmd >}}

## Interaction model {.smaller}

{{< include _subfiles/Linear-models-overview/_sec_hers_lm_interact.qmd >}}

## Stratified model {.smaller}

{{< include _subfiles/Linear-models-overview/_sec_hers_lm_stratified.qmd >}}

## Goodness of fit {.smaller}

{{< include _subfiles/Linear-models-overview/_sec_hers_lm_gof.qmd >}}

## Diagnostics {.smaller}

### Parallel-planes model

{{< include _subfiles/Linear-models-overview/_sec_hers_lm_diagnostics.qmd >}}

### Interaction model

{{< include _subfiles/Linear-models-overview/_sec_hers_lm_diagnostics_lm2.qmd >}}

## Model selection {.smaller}

{{< include _subfiles/Linear-models-overview/_sec_hers_lm_model_selection.qmd >}}
